text:p.text:span: saying (the words) of Aradmu, your servant:
while I was on an expedition to Subir,
to firmly secure the taxes on the frontier territory,
to thoroughly investigate the state of (this) frontier,
to confer (with the elites of Subir) about Apilaåa, the high commissioner, and have them come to agreement,
so that he could bring to them (i.e., the Subir elites) up-to-date instructions
Alternative translation: so that as part of the daily instructions he could bring them (the people of Subir) good news.
When I stood at the gate of the (local) palace,
no one inquired about my king’s well-being,
no one rose from (his) seat for me, nor did anyone prostrate himself (before me);
constructed by means of finely combed fleece (panels stretched between)
staked poles inlaid with gold, silver, carnelian, and lapis-lazuli covering an area of thirty
He was all decked out in precious stones and metals.
He sat on a throne on a dais placed over a fine carpet,
and had set his feet on a golden footstool.
He would not remove his feet in my presence!
He had stationed no less than five thousand of his
guards to his right and left.
(He ordered) six grass-fed oxen and sixty fattened sheep placed (on tables) for a meal;
and he took over the performance of my king’s cleansing rites.
No one bothered to bring me in through the very gate at which no-one had inquired about your (well-being).
someone brought me a chair with red gold encrusted knobs and told me: “Sit!”
I answered him: “When I am on my king’s orders I stand—I do not sit!”
two grass-fed oxen and twenty grass-fed sheep onto my table,
but even though I had given no offense, my king’s (own) guardsmen overturned my table.
I was so frightened that my skin crawled!
My king, you gave me orders the evening of the fifth day of the Ninazu Festival Month;
I am sending you a courier the evening of the first day of the Swan-Eating Month.
It is midday, and war is brewing!
Now my king is informed (about all of this)!

office:text.text:p: Although this was the most commonly studied letter in the OB schools, it is full of lexical and syntactic difficulties. For this reason, but also because this translation differs from all previous ones, including a number of my own, a detailed commentary is required.

text:p.text:span: . A constant problem that has come up in working with the CKU has been the rendition of
), literally, “my lord,” or “my king.” This occurs most frequently in the address phrase of letter addressed to kings:
. For Akkadian speaking scribes this corresponded, almost morpheme for morpheme, to the corresponding letter formula
, as discussed above. It is quite possible that the Sumerian phrase is actually a calque from Akkadian; nevertheless,
means more than “my king” in Ur III Sumerian. In administrative texts from Drehem the name of the living king is almost never invoked, except to refer to statues. Rather, the term that is used is
, even in third-person reference; correspondingly, often finds
), literally “my queen/mistress,” when the queen is mentioned (P. Michalowski,
88–89); it is important to remember that in the political language of the Ur III state, with a few exceptions that need not concern us here, there was only one real
in the mundane world, and that was the sovereign of Ur; all other rulers were described as
, as were the provincial and city governors of the state itself. At the same time, the word
can be used, more simply, to mean “master,” much like Akkadian

, for the master of a slave, for example. From this one may infer that the phrase is an important formal manner of address, and I have struggled with the translations, going back and forth between “my liege,” “his majesty,” “Milord,” and simply “my king.” For subjective aesthetic reasons I have settled on the latter, although stylistically, and even perhaps ideologically, this is perhaps not the best solution.
, “road, expedition,” corresponds here to the Akkadian idiomatic use of
in the meaning, “to proceed, march on.” The rare ending
found on nominalized verbal forms has been discussed by J. Krecher,
57 (1965) 27, and a host of others, most recently by Christopher Woods. Both suggest that it serves to topicalize or stress the end of subordinate clauses and also
has a secondary temporal or spatial meaning, hence the translation “while I was….”
Christopher Woods, “The Element -re and the Organization of Erim-æuå,” paper read at the 213
annual meeting of the American Oriental Society, A
Woods argues that this is still another function of the demonstrative –re.
and on the poetic structure of the opening lines see above p. 000.
requires comment. In Sumerian the noun
“times (mathematical)” as well as “customary way, manner of behavior.” Its Akkadian equivalent,
, has a broader meaning, semantically parallel to English “way,” as already in Old Assyrian and Old Babylonian it can also refer to “road, way, passage, etc.” Students would have known the expression
“I am a scribe who knows the ways of Nidaba.” Closer to the general semantic context of the letter is the use found in the

office:text.text:p: (Ur­-Namma,) the stand of cypress trees was struck down; the mood of the homeland was confused,

text:p.text:span: The juniper tree of the homeland was overturned; the mood of the homeland was altered.
Here, as in our passage, the expression appears to be a synonym of
There is only one other example of the Sumerian expression with duplication of the verbal root: Waradsin 29 59 [E4.2.13.21]
people of the conditions of my kingship.” This usage is what one would expect:

“to teach,” but that can hardly apply here, and therefore it is most probable that the duplication is intensive: “to thoroughly investigate.” However, in view of the high level of underlying Akkadian syntax and semantics in some of the CKU letters, it is also highly probable that the present line was also understood, by some at least, as “to investigate the roads through the frontier;” in fact, a double meaning may very well be intentional. Indeed, it is possible that
may be a calque from Akkadian
, which has been thoroughly analyzed by Tzvi Abusch,
Text X1 has additional material which may perhaps be restored as:
, “to secure the foundations of the land.”
. This line is unique; it has caused problems for all who have worked on this text, and remains open to different interpretations. The verb is probably
, “to bring news,” discussed by M. Civil,
25 92. Since this is a calque of
the syntax of the phrase has to be understood as Akkadian. The noun is qualified by
. One could understand this in an adverbial manner, as in
, “daily expenses/wages/work,” for which see R. de Maaijer and B. Jagersma,
in Ukg 6 iii 20’ and 23’, for which see D. O. Edzard,
, 19, who notes that this is an adjectival compound. The plural form here is what drives the translation of lines 6–7; the force of
in line 6 is difficult to gauge and were it not for this plural form, one would consider the rendition “to council (with) Apila
a and come to mutual agreement.”
, “to send news, command, instruct.” In CKU it is almost always construed with /
/ on the first element. To my knowledge, this idiosyncratic usage is not attested elsewhere; for the most part OB Sumerian utilizes
. This element must be the deictic particle /
/, and the use of it here probably reflects
, “now, here.” Students in OB schools would have been familiar with the rhetorical use of the verb governing purpose clauses from the elementary exercise
(part of the “tetrad”). The first person ergative agreement marker, usually 0 in classical Sumerian, is here expressed by
Note the intentionally ambiguous use of the term

a’s residence. On one level Aradmu is implying the usurpation of royal power, anticipating the lines that follow; rhetorically, the line really means “when I had arrived at his residence that was a veritable royal palace.” On a more prosaic level, the word
can be used to designate roadside establishments as well as royal palaces; apprentice scribes would know this from their study of the ubiquitous hymn
, in which the king boasts that he built roadside establishments, or caravan serai (
), for travelers along the roads of Sumer (line 29). Similar ambiguous rhetoric is probably found in the final part of
; the goddess predicts that after his death Åukaletuda will be remembered in songs performed in royal palaces (line 298:
) but that he will dwell in the caravanserai of the open desert (line 301:
). Note that there were at least seven placed designated as
in the Umma province alone in Ur III times (P. Steinkeller,
193). There is one Ur III document from Nippur that mentions an
1–2 174: 7–8). Since Tumal did not have its own governor, this must refer to the temporary residence of the man who ran Nippur; see also, possibly the Girsu document

office:text.text:p: 10. This expression has a very specific technical meaning in this context. silim-ma is not “hail,” or “bon santé,” but a very specific obligation to inquire about the king’s well-being and safety. It seems to allude to a blessing that was used in the time of Åulgi that might be idiomatically rendered as “Long Live the King.” The wording of this blessing is explicitly provided in ArÅ4: 6–7 (letter 8): silim-ma lugal-Ñá-ke4 èn mu-tar-re-eå lugal-me u4 da-rí-åè hé-ti gù bí-in-≠dé-eå, “they inquired about my king’s well-being and shouted out ‘Long Live the King!’” Almost the same wording is found in a subscript written at the end of the Ur III version of the Sumerian King List (which ends with the reign of Ur-Namma): ≠dåul-gi lugal-Ñu10 u4 sud-åè ≠ha-ti-il, “May Åulgi, my king, live a life of long days!” (P. Steinkeller, Fs. Wilcke 274). This official blessing is also encoded in OB personal names such as Hamatil, Ušareš-hetil (Mari and Ešnunna), and Til-ani-hesud (Akk. Balassu-lirik; see, most recently, D. Charpin, OBO 160/4 262, idem., Hammurapi 142–43). In Ur III we commonly find RN/lugal/nin/PN-ha-ma-ti, and once dÅu-dSin-hé-ti (CUAS 88:3). This was obviously a common element of royal protocol, as evidenced by a passage in a letter from Iamum to Zimri-Lim in Mari: “the entire army cried out ‘Long Live Our King’ (bur bêlī)!” (ARM 26/2 327 2’–3’); the same cry was addressed to Hammurabi, according to ARM 26/2 363 5–10; see also J. M. Durand, LAPO 17 9d.

text:p.text:span: This use of the term is also attested in an Ur III document from the Drehem “treasury”; in
, “because he brought the salutations of (Queen) Abi-simti from Uruk.”
. To my knowledge, the idiom, or compound verb,
is otherwise unattested. Compare the commonly attested

. The reading of this line is not clear, and it appears that it already offered problems to the ancients so that some Nippur students solved the problem by omitting it altogether. Every text has a different version of this line. With reservations I base my interpretation on
, “to be or become restless or nervous,” or
in N9 and X3 reflect aural misinterpretations. The use of
, “silence, torpor,” for which see now M. Jaques,
217, is clearly related. One assumes a rhetorical progress; here Aradmu is very worried but after the rest of the story he is absolutely terrified (line 30).
. It is difficult to ascertain the exact meaning of the rare
, and only twice in Ur III. The first is an incomplete Umma text (
93:2); the second is an account from Urusagrig that includes provisions for a royal messenger,
, “when he traveled to prepare the royal
” (LH 5458 8–9, courtesy D. I. Owen). Note that the scribe of N1 was thinking of the more common
but thought better of it and complemented the term with

stands for a lance or spear for war and hunting but which can also have other uses, more specifically as a pole or stake to create fencing, in our case to hold the fleece panels. This is clearly the case in
the Lamentation over the Destruction of Sumer and Ur
, “that the shepherd(s) not enclose the sacred sheep fold with a fence.” The image of a fence made up of fleece panels held up by metal poles is what lies behind the otherwise impenetrable passage in

office:text.text:p: Lions (trapped) by copper stakes are cut off like leeks,

text:p.text:span: (trapped) by fleece panels are broken like reeds,
Their life is … like dogs by an axe.
This is the meaning also implied by Diri Nippur 145 (
This must be the same word as
H 57 as a Hurrian loan with distribution limited to Nuzi.

office:text.text:p: 17. The compound verb mí…dug4 has many nuances; see P. Attinger, Eléments 603–19. On p. 612 he reads the line as [na4 (?)] ≠za?-gin3? ku3-sig17 ku3-babbar ≠na4gug mi2 zi-de-eå3 im-me, and proposes a translation: “Il (Apillaša) ne (prend soin comme il se droit=) s’intéresse vraiment qu’au lapis, à l’or, à l’argent et à la cornaline.” We differ in the reading of the line but I would not completely rule out his interpretation. The verb is mí zi-dè-eå…dug4 and corresponds to Akk. kīniš kunnu, “to honor.” The translation of the syntactic connections in lines 16–19 reflects the use of the non-perfect (17) followed by perfect (18 and 19).

text:p.text:span: , all with the meaning “finest quality cloth/garment;” see M. Green,
is adopted here in view of the complement
in U1. This does not mean that all the other students who studied this text understood it in the same manner. It is also possible that some scribes understood this to be a royal robe, as in

“after (King) Ibbi-Sin had donned the special robe, the ‘mountain range’ robe …” Thus the line would mean, “sat himself on a throne, dressed in a special (royal) robe.” There is another meaning of the word, however, which is probably more pertinent to the present passage. As W. Heimpel,
a tent, or perhaps an awning.
follows the traditional interpretation, strengthened by Heimpel’s definition
,” (unpublished communication read July 21, 2005 at the RAI in Chicago) has argued that
this was really a large ceremonial tent; in that case one would have to translate “he sat inside a (ceremonial) tent under an awning.”
together in an economic document, see
is used to designate an object that is set under foot; in connection with thrones it designates a footstool. D. Soubeyran,
23 333, draws attention to the representation of such footstools on cylinder seals, where they belong to deities and kings. This illustrates well the symbolic implications of Apilaåa’s actions.
. The high number of guards is undoubtedly hyperbolic and should not be taken literally, although there are examples of very large number of
in Ur III texts (Lafont, CDLJ 2009/5); for the meaning of the term see
. The use of the Semitic loan for “one thousand” has thus far only been attested in literary royal correspondence (ArÅ7: 8 [7], Ni. 4164 [
], SEpM 8–10). There is an Ur III text from Nippur that mentions 6
1 318: 1) but it is possible that this was actually written in Akkadian.
is rare and difficult to define properly. The earliest attestation known to me is in the seal inscription of one
4, E4.3.7.2004). The translation is based on the lexical equation

The translation “no less than” seeks to render the force of the copula; I have assume that the verbal form is transitive but it is possible, in view of the reinterpretations of the use of Sumerian shifters that are characteristic of many forms in the literary letters, that it is intransitive and means simply “stood (pl.).”
. It is difficult to understand the use of
in this context. In view of the change of tone in the following line, it is possible that Apila
a performs the ritual on behalf of the welfare of the king. More probable is that in this series of implied usurpations of royal power, Apila
a is accused of performing rituals that properly belong to the king, as in
mu-tag åu-luh nam-lugal-lá-ka åu gal mu-ni-du
ched (the Etemenniguru) and magnificently perfected it with royal
. purification rites,” and many similar passages where these purification rites are the prerogative of deities and kings. P. Attinger,
96, is clearly thinking in this direction when he renders this line as: “
a) s’est arrogé les rites de purification de mon roi
purification needs a fuller study. These are clearly major rites and seem linked with dining. This is well illustrated by the passage in

office:text.text:p: And performed the ritual hand washing;
Then my properly prepared hands woke holy An;
The sweet morsels from my hands

The association between iåib an-na and royalty is attested for Lipit-Eštar Hymn A: 23, and, more to the point here, for Rim-Sin Hymn E: 1–2: [...] x x [...] åu-luh kù-ga túm-ma [dri-im]-dsin iåib an-na sízkur sikil-la túm-ma, “… fit for (performing) the holy š. rites, Rim-Sin, purification priest of An, fit for (performing) the pure å. rites.”

text:p.text:span: The word for “gate” ends in /-n/; I understand the opening phrase as a complex anticipatory genitive,
. On the Sumerian word for gate, probably /
14 41] and in X8), see M. Civil,
here; it is not a high officer who asks him to enter but a mere nobody.
The reading and meaning of the verb is uncertain. The choice of
is based on N2 and Ur1; but other sources have
but the final sign is broken in a number of manuscripts. X7 seems to have
but the remains are ambiguous. The meaning “heap up” of
is based on the usage found in
, “May the foreign land of Meluhha load precious desirable cornelian, perfect
wood into large ships for you,” and futher down in
. This first verb in this line has troubled previous editors. The interpretation offered here is based on
in N6, which seems to be in harmony with the lexical entries such as
12 208]). One could also take into consideration a reading
, and translate the line: “Before I could rise and depart, my king’s (own) guardsmen overturned my table.” A remote possibility is that
, in the meaning “to be bloated, full” (“although I was not yet satiated….”).
The final verbal form is problematical. The only complete form is
in N1, not the most reliable source. This suggests a causative construction. The admittedly unsatisfactory composite version follows Ur1 and X1.
. Students could not have ignored the instructive parallel with the ubiquitous
, “but because I am King, I have absolutely no fear!” Aradmu is clearly indicating his subordinate rank. The two almost synonymous verbs
are used in a traditional hendiadys as a rhetorical figure, hence the translation.

. The final lines of the text are different in two manuscripts, N1 and Ur1, which are included in the matrix but also transliterated separately. It is obvious that the Old Babylonian scribes had difficulties with Ur III menology and were not sure of the writing, nor of the order of the month names; the Ur student seems to have had the most difficulties. Within the official state calendar the Swan-Eating Month (
was month three (four after ÅS 3 and at Ur), and the Ninazu Festival Month (
was month five (six after ÅS 3 and at Ur) but both names were out of use by
the OB period. Note that all preserved Nippur sources omit the bird determinative and at least one (N1) writes
For the probable identification of the bird as a swan, see P. Steinkeller
For a similar formulation in an OB letter from Mari, see
, “at the end of the fourteenth day of the month Kiskissum I am sending this tablet to my king.”
. This is the only occurrence of a
CKU; these were simple couriers, who carried messages, as distinguished from the
, who are also brought oral instructions and possibly read the letters aloud to the recipient. This corresponds to the Old Babylonian distinction between
176. Note that in Ur III archival texts the former do not occur as frequently as the latter.

The verbal form must be understood as the Sumerian equivalent of the “epistolary perfect;” it refers to the performative nature of the action. For the concept, as applied to other ancient Near Eastern languages, see D. Pardee and R. M. Whiting,

. This line, which is broken in every witness, is crucial for the understanding of the final passage. There are one or two signs missing in all manuscripts, and the only clue to the missing word is found in text N6, which reads:
; the traces in Ur1 are also congruent with this reading. The signs
have been discussed by P. Steinkeller,
20 (1981) 243–49 but the meaning here is elusive. Lexical texts provide the Akkadian equivalent
, “battle, quarrel, strife,” but in literary and historical texts
this is usually rendered by the reduplicated form
; see, for a rare unreduplicated example,
The Ur1 text has a unique variant for the beginning of the line,
; it is possible that this is related to the likewise unique, if difficult, Ur ending of
Photo on line at CDLI (http://cdli.ucla.edu/dl/photo/P247912.jpg). The tablet has deteriorated somewhat since it was copied by D. Foxvog and since I collated it (formerly UCLM 9-1815).

text:note-body.text:p: This is not, properly speaking, a source of the letter. These are lines that were used to create a new one together with elements of the answer, and it is edited here as ŠAr1a. The appropriate lines are incorporated into the matrix for comparative purposes.

text:p.text:span: X2 possibly from Sippar, X3 and X7 possibly from Larsa. The Tutub provenience of X6 is doubtful (see p.
Uk1, N1, and X4 collated on photographs (collations of X4 also by H. Waetzoldt,

text:section.text:p: Tablet typology: compilation tablets: N1, N3, Ur1, Ur2, X1, X2. The rest are all Type III, except X7 (one column with two letters) and X4 (oval exercise tablets with excerpts of letters 1 and 2).
X3 . . o o o o o
N3 o o o o o o o . . + o o o . +
N9 . o o o o o o o o o o . + + .
X1 o o o o o o o o o . . + + + +
X3 . . . . . . . . o o o o o o o
N3 o o o o o . + + +
N9 + o o o o o o o .
N3 o o o o o . +
X2 o o o o o o .

text:note-body.text:p: Probably to be read [suhuš] ≠ma-da ge-≠ne-dè, borrowed from ŠAr1 7 and 33 (letter 3).

text:note-body.text:p: Possibly read ≠te-e at the beginning of the line. There does not seem to be enough room for line 12.

text:note-body.text:p: There are remnants of two signs and space for two more at the­ beginning of this line; probably had end of 16.

N4 + + + . o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: It is unclear if this is a true phonetic variant, or a graphic error for sá. This scribe had an idiosyncratic hand, and also sometimes used this shape for åè.

text:section.text:p: X3 o o o o o o o o x
X5 + + x . o o o o o o
N2 + . o o o o o o o o o o o o o
N4 + + + + o o o o o o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: There is a small vertical line in the middle of the sign that almost makes it look like zu but collation suggests that it is not a real wedge. There does not seem to be room at the beginning of the line for more than one sign.

text:section.text:p: N6 + + + . o o o o o o

text:p.text:span: o o o o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: The scribe wrote a sign, possibly ha, erased it, and then wrote an incomplete mu.

text:section.text:p: X7 o o + o o o o o o o
N2 + . oo o o o o o o o

text:p.text:span: o o o o o o

text:section.text:p: N7 o o oo o o o . + + +
X5 . o oo o o o o o o o
N2 + . o o o o o
N7 o o o o o .in+
N7 o o o o o o +
X7 o o o o o . + :
Three manuscripts have different line orders:

text:p.text:span: “I am sending you this messenger the evening of the fifth day of the
My king, you gave me orders the evening of the first day of the Swan-Eating Month. Now it is midday, and war is brewing. Now my king is informed (about all of this)!”
The scribe had some problems at this point. The sign is really
, followed by an erasure; the following
“My king gave me orders when the fifteenth day of the Ninazu Festival Month approached. On the first day of the
It is not yet the middle of the day
and war is brewing. Now my king is informed (about all of this)!”
“My king, you gave me orders the evening of the first day of the Swan-Eating Month. I am sending you this messenger the evening of the x-th day of the
. Now it is midday, and war is brewing. Now my king is informed (about all of this)!”
